questions.question: A faculty member records a 30-minute lecture and uploads it to YouTube, which auto-generates captions. The faculty member enables the auto-captions and makes them available to students. Does this satisfy WCAG 2.1 AA requirements for caption accessibility?

options.text: No — auto-generated captions must be reviewed and edited by a human to verify accuracy, especially for technical terminology and proper nouns
Yes — YouTube's auto-captions are 99% accurate and meet all WCAG requirements
No — only professional captioning services meet WCAG requirements
Yes — as long as the captions are turned on by default and students can see them

questions.explanation: Auto-generated captions are a fast, free starting point, but they typically achieve only 80–90% accuracy. Technical terminology, proper nouns, accents, and background noise cause errors. Industry standards (like DCMP) require 99%+ accuracy for educational content. Auto-captions must be reviewed and edited before they are considered accessible. YouTube Studio makes this editing straightforward.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a transcript and a caption?

options.text: A transcript is a complete text version of audio content (not synchronized with the video), while a caption is synchronized text displayed on screen during playback
A transcript is used for audio-only content, while captions are only for video
A transcript includes only dialogue, while a caption includes sound descriptions like [applause]
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Transcripts and captions serve different purposes. A caption is synchronized with video/audio (it appears at the moment corresponding dialogue is spoken). A transcript is a standalone text document that can be read anytime, independent of the video. Both are required for video content under WCAG — captions for real-time accessibility during playback, transcripts as a complete alternative format.
